coredata.dc:title: Quantitative mixed-valence state identification of metal ions based on fluorescence response of graphene quantum dots

coredata.dc:description: Quantitative identification of mixed-valence metal ions is essential for gaining deeper insights into critical chemical and biological processes in environmental science, chemical engineering, and biological systems. However, a simple approach of quantitative identification mixed-valence metal ions in solution has remained a challenge. In this study, we have experimentally observed a significant linear correlation (R2 = 0.99) between the concentration of high-valence metal ions (using iron ions as an example) and the fluorescence intensity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Utilizing the distinct fluorescence responses of GQDs to high-valence and low-valence metal ions, reliable quantitative detection of mixed-valence metal ions has been successfully achieved. Remarkably, we introduced real-time monitoring of mixed-valence metal ions, revealing a shift from a molar ratio of approximately 4.0 to 2.0. Density functional theory calculations have revealed significant differences in charge transfer between high-valence and low-valence states of metal ions adsorbed onto GQDs. Furthermore, the versatility of this method can extend to various types of GQDs and metal ions, highlighting its universal applicability. This work presents a simple, convenient, and cost-effective approach for quantitatively identifying mixed-valence metal ions in solution, offering a new avenue and opportunity for applications in biochemistry, environmental science, catalysis and materials science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mixed-valence state identification
Metal ions
Graphene quantum dots
Density functional theory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of GQDs to mixed-valence iron ions
Real-time monitoring the dynamics in mixed-valence states of Fe3+/Fe2+ ions
Extensive fluorescence response for mixed-valence states of metal ions
Theoretical calculations for interaction and charge transfer between metal ions and graphene sheet

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a. TEM image of e-GQDs. b. AFM image of e-GQDs with corresponding height profiles taken along the marked white line. c.The FL spectra at excitation wavelengths from 310 nm to 380 nm. d.The FL quenching ratio ((F0-F)/F0) of 1.2 mg/mL e-GQDs in the presence of 0.1 mM Fe3+ with time. e. UV–vis absorption spectra of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution (the background of pure water was deducted). f. The maximum excitation spectra of GQDs, H2O and Fe3+ at 340 nm. g. The maximum emission spectra of GQDs, H2O and Fe3+ at 430 nm.

FL emission spectra of e-GQDs with FeCl3 solution at concentrations of 0.01–1.5 mM. The insets depict photographs of the e-GQDs under 365 nm ultraviolet light (left), and the linear dependence of the FL quenching ratio ((F0-F)/F0) on the logarithm of the FeCl3 concentration (right).

High sensitivity and fast fluorescence response of GQDs to mixed-valence Fe3+/Fe2+. a. Schematic of identification of mixed-valence metal ions in solution based on the fluorescence response of GQDs, using mixed-valence Fe3+/Fe2+ as an example. b. Relative FL ratio (F/F0) of e-GQDs with mixed FeCl3 + FeCl2, FeCl3, and FeCl2 solutions of various concentrations. c. Concentrations of FeCl3 and FeCl2 in mixed solutions obtained by the fitting function, compared to the actual concentration (green and blue residual of inset: standard deviation between predicted concentration and actual concentration).

Real-time monitoring the dynamics in mixed-valence states of iron ions triggered by the redox process based on the fluorescence response of e-GQDs. a. Relative FL ratio of e-GQDs in a 3 mL 1.0 mM FeCl3 solution recovered with the addition of ascorbic acid (AA) (resulting in Fe3+ partially reducing to Fe2+; n denotes the molar ratio). b. Concentration of FeCl3 and FeCl2 in mixed solutions obtained using the relative FL ratio. The reaction of n·Fe3+ + AA → n·Fe2+ with different molar ratios (n) are labelled, respectively. The progress of the reaction at 90 % is indicated as the two shaded areas.

Extensive fluorescence response for mixed-valence states of metal ions. a. Relative FL ratios (F/F0) of e-GQDS with FeCl3, FeCl2, CrCl3, CrCl2, CuCl2, and CuCl solutions having corresponding concentrations of 1.0, 1.0, 20.0, 20.0, 10.0, and 10.0 mM, respectively. b. Relative FL ratios (F/F0) of three functionalised GQDs (e-GQDs, o-GQDs, and n-GQDs) with single FeCl3 and FeCl2 solutions.

Theoretical calculations for interaction between metal ions and GQDs. Stable optimised geometries of metal ions on C54, the partial charge transfers from GQDs to metal ions, natural energy decomposition analysis (NEDA) of the binding energy between metal ions and C54. a and b for Fe3+/Fe2+, c and d for Cr3+/Cr2+, e and f for Cu2+/Cu+. The binding energy was decomposed into electrical interaction (EL, blue bar), charge transfer (CT, green bar), and core repulsion (Core, yellow bar) parts.
UV–vis absorption spectra of GQDs, GQDs + Fe2+ and GQDs + Fe3+ (the background of pure water, Fe3+ and Fe2+ have been deducted).

head.ce:title: Quantitative mixed-valence state identification of metal ions based on fluorescence response of graphene quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Carbon Cycling in Forest Ecosystems and Carbon Sequestration of Zhejiang Province

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantitative identification of mixed-valence metal ions in solution by the distinctive fluorescence responses of graphene quantum dots.

Quantitative identification of mixed-valence metal ions is essential for gaining deeper insights into critical chemical and biological processes in environmental science, chemical engineering, and biological systems. However, a simple approach of quantitative identification mixed-valence metal ions in solution has remained a challenge. In this study, we have experimentally observed a significant linear correlation (R2 = 0.99) between the concentration of high-valence metal ions (using iron ions as an example) and the fluorescence intensity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Utilizing the distinct fluorescence responses of GQDs to high-valence and low-valence metal ions, reliable quantitative detection of mixed-valence metal ions has been successfully achieved. Remarkably, we introduced real-time monitoring of mixed-valence metal ions, revealing a shift from a molar ratio of approximately 4.0 to 2.0. Density functional theory calculations have revealed significant differences in charge transfer between high-valence and low-valence states of metal ions adsorbed onto GQDs. Furthermore, the versatility of this method can extend to various types of GQDs and metal ions, highlighting its universal applicability. This work presents a simple, convenient, and cost-effective approach for quantitatively identifying mixed-valence metal ions in solution, offering a new avenue and opportunity for applications in biochemistry, environmental science, catalysis and materials science.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mixed-valence metal ions, the same metal ions with varying valence states, play pivotal roles in numerous critical chemical and biological processes within environmental and biological systems. The quantitative identification of these mixed-valence metal ions can offer invaluable insights into the oxidation states, electronic structures, and chemical activation processes within materials, catalysts, and biological systems, facilitating applications in fields such as biological proteins [1,2], mixed-valence single-atom catalysts [3], magnetic materials [4,5], lithium batteries [6,7], and perovskites [8].

Conventional techniques for identifying metal ions in different valence states include X-ray absorption spectroscopy [5,9,10] X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy [11], electron energy loss spectroscopy [6,12], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [13], chemical titration method [14], and density functional theoretical calculation [3,8,10]. However, these well-established methods often exhibit inherent limitations, encompassing semi-quantitative assessment of microregions under ultra-vacuum, protracted analysis durations, substantial equipment costs, and intricate sample pre-treatment procedures [6,9–12,14–18]. These limitations become particularly pronounced when dealing with the intricate task of identifying mixed-valence metal ions, where dynamic redox transformations occur in solution environments during chemical reactions and within living systems [19,20]. Consequently, there exists a pressing need to develop a simple, convenient, and cost-effective approach for the quantitative identification of mixed-valence metal ions in solution.

Fluorescence detection has emerged as an attractive method visualising the distribution and concentration of single or multiple metal ions due to its high sensitivity and strong selectivity [20–25]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), nanometer-sized and atom-thick graphene oxide, have garnered significant attention as superior fluorophores for bioimaging and optical sensing of metal ions, owing to their excellent photostability, tunable photoluminescence, good water solubility, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and ease of cellular internalization [19,20,26–32]. Previous studies have indicated that high-valence metal ions often exhibit notably stronger interaction with graphene-based materials compared to low-valence metal ions [27,33–35], suggesting that GQDs may exhibit high sensitivity to metal ions with varying valences. Inspired by this, we speculate whether simple fluorescence detection using GQDs can enable the quantitative identification of mixed-valence metal ions in solution environments.

In this work, we proposed a simple, convenient, and cost-effective approach for quantitatively identifying mixed-valence metal ions in solutions. We experimentally established a significant linear correlation (R2 = 0.99) between the concentration of high-valence metal ions, with iron ions as an illustrative example, and the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. Finally, through the distinctive fluorescence responses of GQDs to high and low-valence metal ions, we successfully achieved reliable quantitative detection of mixed-valence metal ions. Importantly, the fluorescence response of the GQDs to different valence states further enabled the real-time monitoring of the dynamic changes in mixed-valence states triggered by the redox process. Moreover, the generality of our proposed method was verified using a variety of typical functionalised GQDs and metal ions. Density functional theory calculations revealed that the high-valence state of metal ions has a considerably stronger charge transfer with GQDs than that of the low-valence state of the same metal ions, yielding the fluorescence response of GQDs to mixed-valence metal ions.

The GQDs were prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal method. Edible starch (0.12 g) was dissolved in ultrapure water (100 mL), stirred at 60 ℃ for 15 min, followed by a hydrothermal reaction at 190 ℃ for 2 h, to prepare GQDs with a concentration of approximately 1.2 mg/mL. The GQDs were examined using TEM (FEI Talos F200C), AFM (Bruker, Multimode-8), and a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hong Kong Tian mei Co., Ltd.).

The chemical reagents CuCl, CuCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, CrCl2, CrCl3·6H2O, and o-GQDs used in our study were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., while AA was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Various solutions were prepared using ultrapure water with an electrical conductivity of 18.2 MΩ.

The hybrid DFT method B3LYP [36–38], with Grimme’s DFT-D3 correction [39] and 6-31G(d) basis set [40], were employed for geometric optimisation and energy calculations. All minima have no virtual frequency, and the energies were corrected using zero-point vibrational energies. In the natural energy decomposition analysis (NEDA) [41–43], the intermolecular interaction energy was proportional to the sum of electrical interaction (EL), core repulsion (CORE), and charge transfer (CT) components. All electronic calculations were performed using the Gaussian 09 program package [44]. The structures were visualised using CYLview [45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of GQDs to mixed-valence iron ions
ce:section.ce:para: The e-GQDs, which are impurity-free and fluorescence-tunable, were synthesised from edible starch using a green one-pot hydrothermal method at a concentration of approximately 1.2 mg/mL [46]. The surface morphology and size distribution of the e-GQDs were characterised using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. The thickness of the e-GQDs, determined by atomic force microscopy (AFM), was approximately 1.0 nm (see Fig. 1b). To assess their fluorescence properties, the fluorescence (FL) spectra of the e-GQDs were measured across excitation wavelengths ranging from 310 nm to 380 nm. As depicted in Fig. 1c, the maximum emission peak occurred at a wavelength of 430 nm when the excitation wavelength was set at 340 nm. Consequently, we selected 340 nm as the excitation wavelength for subsequent fluorescence detection experiments. For mixed-valence mental ions, we initially chose iron ions (Fe3+ and Fe2+) as an illustrative example. We first investigated the correlation between the fluorescence intensity of e-GQDs and Fe3+ ions. To achieve this, we added 20 μL of 15 mM FeCl3 solution to 3 mL of e-GQDs solution. Subsequently, mixture was rapidly analysed at 1-minute intervals using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 1d, a rapid fluorescence quenching occurred within the first minute, followed by stable fluorescence over the subsequent 20 min, indicating a fast response of e-GQDs to Fe3+ ions. It is worth noting that the absorption peaks of Fe3+ (1 mM) are observed around 220 nm and 290 nm, while the maximum excitation peak of e-GQDs at 340 nm and the maximum emission peak at 430 nm are significantly higher than those of pure water and Fe3+ (see Fig. 1e-1 g). Consequently, the potential competitive absorption of the excitation and/or emission wavelength of Fe3+ can be excluded in this work.

To investigate the correlation between the fluorescence intensity of e-GQDs and Fe3+ ion concentration, we introduced different concentration gradients of FeCl3 solution into the GQDs solution, ranging from 0 to 1.5 mM, with specific concentrations set at 0, 0.015 mM, 0.03 mM, 0.045 mM, 0.06 mM, 0.075 mM, 0.09 mM, 0.15 mM, 0.3 mM, 0.45 mM, 0.6 mM, 0.75 mM, 0.9 mM, 1.0 mM, and 1.5 mM. Rapid analysis of the mixed solutions of Fe3+ ions and e-GQDs was conducted using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, and the FL spectra of 3 mL e-GQD solutions containing Fe3+ ions in the range of 0.015–1.5 mM are presented in Fig. 2 . At a concentration of 0.015 mM, the quenching rate was 0.13. As the concentration of Fe3+ increased, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased, reaching a high quenching rate of 0.90 at 1.5 mM. This observation indicates a significant influence of Fe3+ ion concentration on the extent of quenching. Further investigation into the relationship between Fe3+ ion concentration and quenching revealed that the FL quenching ratio ((F0 -F)/F0 ) of e-GQDs exhibited a linear correlation with the logarithm of Fe3+ concentration, with a mean squared error of 0.9924 (inset of Fig. 2). Therefore, the Fe3+ concentration can be inferred from the fitting function using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence quenching of e-GQDs even occurred at 0.01 mM, showing a high sensitivity to Fe3+ ions.

Next, we assessed the fluorescence response of e-GQDs to mixed-valence Fe3+/Fe2+ solutions and compared it to the responses in single Fe2+ and Fe3+ solutions. The concentrations of single Fe2+ and Fe3+ in e-GQDs solutions were 0.01 mM, 0.03 mM, 0.06 mM, 0.1 mM, 0.3 mM, 0.6 mM, and 1.0 mM. The mixed-valence Fe3+/Fe2+ solutions were prepared by combining Fe2+ and Fe3+ in reverse order, such as 0.01 mM Fe3+ mixed with 1.0 mM Fe2+. This effectively simulated various ion compositions commonly found in real mixed solutions, including scenarios with a higher concentration of Fe3+ ions, a higher concentration of Fe2+ ions, and situations where the concentrations of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions are close. As depicted in Fig. 3 b, when the concentration of Fe3+ ions was higher (1 mM), the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of Fe3+/Fe2+ mixed solutions was significantly quenched, with an F/F0 value of 0.15. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was noticeably quenched in the presence of 1 mM Fe3+ ions alone, with an F/F0 value of 0.14. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence of 1 mM Fe2+ ions alone showed minimal quenching, with an F/F0 value of 0.87. The relative fluorescence intensities of the single Fe2+ system was nearly unchanged even at 1.0 mM concentration. The mixed-valence system showed the same order of relative fluorescence intensities as the single Fe3+ system, indicating a selective response to Fe2+ or Fe3+.

Based on the above experimental observation, the fast, sensitive, and selective response to Fe3+ in a mixed Fe3+/Fe2+ system may potentially quantify the two valence states in a mixed system. Thus, according to the fitting function depicted in Fig. 2, we calculated the predicted Fe3+ and Fe2+ concentrations in the mixed solutions and compared them to their actual concentrations using a plot of logarithms, as shown in Fig. 3c. The results revealed that the predicted concentrations of Fe3+ and Fe2+ were consistent with their actual concentrations. The inset of Fig. 3c displays the residuals between predicted and actual values, with green and blue residuals representing the standard deviations between predicted and actual concentrations for Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions, respectively. The results indicate that the residuals for the predicted concentration of Fe3+ ions compared to their actual concentration were less than 0.2, while the residuals for the predicted concentration of Fe2+ ions compared to their actual concentration were less than 0.1. For high concentrations of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions (>0.1 mM, logC < -1.0), the overlap between predicted and actual concentrations was the highest, with residuals between predicted and actual values as low as 0.001. This suggests that the method accurately quantifies mixed-valence metal ions when the ion concentration ranges from 0.1 mM to 1 mM. Note that our quantitative method for Fe3+ and Fe2+ here, required the total iron concentration, which could be easily measured using traditional methods, such as inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) [13,15,16].

To confirm that the observed fluorescence quenching was not influenced by anions, we conducted two experiments. Firstly, we replicated the experiments using 1 mM Fe2(SO4)3 and FeSO4 salts. As shown in Fig. S3a, similar to FeCl3 and FeCl2 systems, e-GQDs with Fe2(SO4)3 solution exhibited fluorescence quenching, while e-GQDs with FeSO4 showed no significant impact on fluorescence intensity. Secondly, we introduced pure NaCl to the e-GQDs solution. Our observations revealed minimal changes in fluorescence, even with NaCl concentrations reaching 1 mM. (see Fig. S3b) These results both affirm that anions do not contribute to the observed fluorescence quenching phenomenon.

Previous studies have shown that pH can influence the optical properties of GQDs [47,48]. Therefore, we conducted further investigations into the potential impact of pH on our system. Initially, the pH tests of both the e-GQDs solution, with and without the presence of Fe2(SO4)3 and FeSO4, FeCl3, and FeCl2, indicated the pH of around ∼ 4. (see Fig. S3a). Subsequently, we systematically adjusted the pH of the pure e-GQDs solution within the range of 2 to 6. As illustrated in Fig. S3b, the fluorescence intensity of e-GQDs at pH 4 remained nearly identical to that of the initial e-GQDs solution. This demonstrates that pH variations are not responsible for the observed fluorescence quenching in e-GQDs with Fe2(SO4)3 and FeCl3.

Based on the above results, our investigations can demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching phenomenon is primarily driven by metal ions. Utilizing the established linear correlation between FL quenching ratio of e-GQDs and Fe3+ concentration, the Fe3+ concentration in mixed-valence iron ions can be directly predicted. Then, by exploiting the differential fluorescence responses of e-GQDs to Fe3+/Fe2+ (as depicted in Fig. 3a), we can indirectly deduce the Fe2+ concentration, thus achieving the quantitative identification of Fe3+/Fe2+ in solution. This methodology allows for the rapid quantitative identification of Fe3+/Fe2+ in solution only through simple fluorescence detection, without the need for intricate pre-treatment processes or costly equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time monitoring the dynamics in mixed-valence states of Fe3+/Fe2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: To further demonstrate the quantitative identification of valence states, we performed a typical redox reaction of ascorbic acid (AA), studied extensively in biological systems [19,49,50], such as, 2·Fe3+ + AA → 2·Fe2+, to monitor the valence states of ions in real-time. We prepared 3 mL e-GQDs solutions containing 1.0 mM Fe3+ ions, and then added AA (50 mM) solutions with negligible volume (5.0–30.0 μL) to these e-GQDs solutions. This resulted in a partial reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. The redox process of Fe3+ to Fe2+ was controlled by the amount of AA added to the e-GQDs solutions. As a control experiment, 30.0 μL of AA solution was added to the e-GQDs solution without Fe3+ ions. Emission spectra of mixtures were collected for 10 min while adding AA.

As shown in Fig. S1, even when the volume of AA was increased to 30 μL, the relative FL ratio (F/F0) remained as high as 0.94, indicating a negligible effect of AA on the fluorescence intensity of e-GQDs. In e-GQD solutions containing 1.0 mM Fe3+ ions, the F/F0 value increased from 0.16 to 0.82 as the volume of AA increased from 5.0 to 30.0 μL. This observation indicates a distinct dynamic change in the oxidation state of Fe3+ ions, which were reduced to Fe2+ ions during the chemical reaction. The corresponding dynamic concentrations of Fe3+ and Fe2+ in real-time were facilely obtained using the relative FL ratio. To investigate the reduction efficiency of AA in the GQDs solution, the relative concentrations of Fe3+ and Fe2+ ions were analyzed. (see Fig. 4 b) During the reaction of n·Fe3+ + AA → n·Fe2+, the molar ratio (n) was approximately 2.0 for the complete reduction of Fe3+ at a concentration of 1.0 mM, consistent with early reports [49,50]. Surprisingly, with the addition of AA, the average of the molar ratio (n) was approximately 4.0 (ranging from 4.8 to 3.6), when the Fe3+ amount was reduced to ≤ 90 %. For the complete reduction of the remaining ∼ 10 % of Fe3+, the amount of AA added was almost half of the total amount, resulting in a final molar ratio (n) close to 2, as depicted in Fig. 4b. We attributed this to the high concentration of Fe2+ ions, allowing oxidation occurred to some extent. Subsequently, the reducing capacity of AA was weakened. Importantly, such detailed and real-time monitoring of the dynamic changes in both the molar ratio of reactants and mixed-valence state of metal ions is key to understanding the underlying mechanisms of chemical and biological processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extensive fluorescence response for mixed-valence states of metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the universality of the detection method proposed in this work, we further conducted an extended analysis encompassing a broader range of identified metal ions and GQDs variations. Firstly, we extend the type of ions to Cr3+/Cr2+, and Cu2+/Cu+, and the concentrations of metal ions in e-GQDs solutions were 1.0, 1.0, 20.0, 20.0, 10.0, and 10.0 mM, respectively. The fluorescence response of e-GQDs to Cr3+/Cr2+, and Cu2+/Cu+ solutions were depicted in Fig. 5 a. For comparison, Fe3+/Fe2+ under the same conditions were also explored. The corresponding fluorescence spectra for each category of metal ions were presented in Fig. S4. As shown in Fig. 5a, like Fe3+/Fe2+ systems, the relative fluorescence intensities of Cr3+ and Cu2+ ions were reduced significantly compared to the corresponding Cr2+ and Cu+ ions, indicating a considerably stronger fluorescence quenching of high-valence metal ions than that of low-valence metal ions for the same metal ions. Clearly, the high selectivity of fluorescence response of e-GQDs are also universal for other kinds of mixed-valence metal ions.

Then, the generality of our proposed method was further verified using a variety of typically functionalised GQDs, such as hydroxylated GQDs (o-GQDs) [51], and nitrogen-doped GQDs (n-GQDs) [52]. The fluorescence response of e-GQDs, o-GQDs, and n-GQDs to 1.0 mM Fe3+ and Fe2+ solutions is shown in Fig. 5b. Similar to e-GQDs, o-GQDs, and n-GQDs also exhibited the different fluorescence response to high- or low-valence states of iron ions, which implies the suitable for various GQDs. Overall, it is reasonable that the approach we proposed here is a simple and university quantitative identification method for mixed-valence metal ions in solution. It should be note that, we observed that the FL quenching ratios are very associated with the types of GQDs and the types of metal ions, hence appropriate GQDs need to be selected or synthesised for an accurate quantitative assessment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculations for interaction and charge transfer between metal ions and graphene sheet

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, our experimental findings have established a significant linear correlation (R2 = 0.99) between Fe3+ concentration and GQD fluorescence intensity. The exceptional selectivity and rapid responses of GQDs to Fe3+/Fe2+ were observed. Utilizing the distinctive fluorescence responses, we successfully achieved the quantitative identification. Significantly, the versatility of this method extends to other types of metal ions (Cr3+/Cr2+ and Cu2+/Cu+) and GQDs (o-GQDs, and n-GQDs), indicating its universality. Furthermore, our study has introduced real-time monitoring capabilities for tracking dynamic changes in the molar ratio of reactants and the mixed-valence state of metal ions. This real-time insight revealed a shift from an initial molar ratio of approximately 4.0 to 2.0 during the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+, providing a comprehensive understanding of the chemical processes involved. Overall, our research presents a simple, cost-effective methodology for the quantitative identification of mixed-valence metal ions in solution. This approach holds promise for examining metal ions in chemical and biological processes, facilitating precise assessments of metal ion dynamics and responses to environmental changes. Its rapid, sensitive, and quantitative nature, combined with its simplicity and the mature, low-cost preparation of GQDs, opens exciting avenues for gaining deeper insights into the underlying mechanisms of chemical and biological processes. This work represents a significant step towards full quantitative identification of mixed-valence metal ions in solution, with potential applications in cell and subcellular analysis, environmental science, chemical reaction control, mixed-valence single-atom catalysts, and material analyses.

L.C. and J.X. conceived the research; J.Y., P.L., J.L., R.Y., J.L., and L.L. performed the experimental studies and analyzed the data; J.X. and Z.W. performed the calculations and analyzed the data; L.C., J.X., and Z.W. wrote the manuscript; J.Y., W.L., J.C., and S.F. helped to revise the manuscript. All authors discussed and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (12075211, 12074341, 11975206), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang (2020TD001), and the Scientific Research and Developed Fund of Zhejiang A&F University (No. 2017FR032).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2023.112465.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Crystal structure of a purple acid phosphatase containing a dinuclear Fe(III)-Zn(II) active site

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Sträter, T. Klabunde, P. Tucker, H. Witzel, B. Krebs, Crystal Structure of a Purple Acid Phosphatase Containing a Dinuclear Fe(III)-Zn(II) Active Site, Science. 268 (1995) 1489–1492.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Oxygen activation and the conservation of energy in cell respiration

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G.T. Babcock, M. Wikström, Oxygen activation and the conservation of energy in cell respiration, Nature. 356 (1992) 301–309.
